author: Sofia Martínez
title: Paid Search on a $500/Month Budget: Service Business Blueprint
description: Stop wasting clicks. We show service businesses how to win local searches with limited ad spend using dayparting, location targeting, and conversion tracking.

sections.p: We've watched service businesses blow through $1,500/month on Google Ads with nothing to show for it. The difference between those failures and the ones generating 3-4 qualified leads weekly? They stopped treating paid search like a spray-and-pray channel. If you're running a plumbing, HVAC, cleaning, or pest control business on a $500–$1,000 monthly budget, you need ruthless focus. Here's exactly how to deploy that money.

Most service businesses lose 30–40% of their ad spend to out-of-area clicks. A roofer in Denver shouldn't pay for clicks from someone 40 miles away. Start by defining a 5–10 mile radius around your service area and set location targeting to "people in or regularly in these locations." If you service multiple neighborhoods, create separate campaigns for each zone. We've seen plumbing companies cut wasted spend by $150/month just by switching from city-level to neighborhood-level targeting.

Use Google's location bid adjustments to increase bids by 15–20% in your most profitable zip codes. If you're a locksmith and emergency calls from downtown generate better margins than suburban calls, boost bids there. Test this over 2–3 weeks before scaling.

sections.h2: Daypart Your Budget Like a Surgeon

sections.p: You don't get emergency calls at 2 a.m., but your ads might be running then anyway. Dayparting means adjusting bids (or pausing entirely) based on time of day and day of week. For most service businesses, Monday–Thursday, 8 a.m.–5 p.m. drives 55–70% of qualified calls. Friday and weekend calls often convert worse because people procrastinate.

list: Night/early morning: Pause entirely or reduce by 50%
Weekends: Bid -15% to -30% unless emergencies are your revenue

sections.p: A cleaning service in Austin cut costs by $180/month just by pausing ads between 6 p.m. and 8 a.m. on weekdays. They got the same number of inquiries, but paid less per click.

sections.h2: Use Exact Match Keywords and Call Extensions

sections.p: Broad match is a budget killer for services. Someone searching "how to fix a leaky faucet" isn't ready to hire a plumber today. Someone searching "emergency plumber near me" is calling within 30 minutes. Shift 70% of your budget to exact-match and phrase-match keywords that include intent words: emergency, near me, today, now, same-day, urgent.

Add click-to-call extensions to every ad. On mobile, 25–35% of clicks for service businesses come directly from the call button, not the website. Make sure your phone number is real and you're answering calls within 2 rings. A missed call is a lost customer at $50–$200 in revenue.

sections.quote: Exact match keywords cost 30–40% less per click than broad match, but only if you're actually bidding on the right words. You need search term reports every week.

sections.p: If your average job is $800 and your margin is 40%, a customer is worth $320. You can't afford to spend more than $50–$80 per qualified lead (that's 15–25% of customer value). Set a max CPC (cost per click) of $2–$4 depending on your service. Monitor conversions daily, not monthly. If your conversion rate drops below 5% (clicks to qualified calls), pause underperforming keywords immediately.

Use Google's conversion tracking to measure phone calls, not just website form submissions. Set up call duration triggers (calls longer than 30 seconds usually mean a real customer). A handyman service in Phoenix cut their CAC from $92 to $64 in 4 weeks by pausing keywords that generated lots of clicks but short, low-quality calls.
